title: Data Update 5 for 2026: Risk and Hurdle Rates
gist: Damodaran's annual global risk/hurdle-rate dataset (48,156 firms) shows utilities and financials as the consistently safest sectors and tech/healthcare/energy as the riskiest across both accounting and price-based measures, while cost of capital shows no clean pattern by company age and 80% of global firms cluster between roughly 6.3% and 11.7%.

insights.claim: Damodaran defines risk as the pairing of danger and opportunity (invoking the Chinese symbol for crisis), arguing this duality means an investment or business strategy cannot rationally be built around simply minimizing risk, since that also cuts off access to upside.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Risk-minimization mandates (e.g., low-vol strategies, overly conservative capital budgeting hurdles) can be self-defeating by screening out legitimate opportunity along with danger.
insights.quote: an investment or business strategy generally cannot be built around the objective of just minimizing risk

insights.claim: Any risk measure choice - upside vs. downside, price-based vs. accounting-based, total vs. non-diversifiable - implicitly encodes an assumption about who the marginal (share-trading) investor is and whether that investor is diversified.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts should pick a risk metric that matches their actual marginal investor base rather than defaulting to whichever measure is most familiar (e.g., beta only makes sense if the marginal investor is diversified).
insights.locator: the risk measure you choose will be a function of whether

insights.claim: Harry Markowitz's Nobel-winning insight - that investment-specific risk averages out in a portfolio while market-wide risk does not - is the theoretical foundation for nearly all modern risk-and-return models, including beta-based frameworks.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Justifies why diversified investors should price only non-diversifiable (market) risk into hurdle rates, ignoring company-specific volatility.
insights.locator: This insight, which earned Harry Markowitz a Nobel prize

insights.claim: Using debt load as a proxy for company risk is misleading because it would misclassify a young, high-growth, debt-free company as low-risk even though its business risk is high.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors screening for 'safe' names via low leverage alone may be systematically mispricing risky, unprofitable growth companies as defensive.
insights.locator: While there are some who use debt loads as proxies

themes.theme: Sector Risk Rankings - Accounting Measures (2025, Global, n=48,156)

insights.claim: Across roughly 48,156 global publicly traded firms, utilities have the least volatile operating income (lowest coefficient of variation), while energy and technology have the most volatile operating income.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Confirms utilities as the structurally lowest-operating-risk sector globally, useful as a baseline when setting sector-relative hurdle rates.
insights.locator: utilities have the least volatile operating earnings

insights.claim: On a net income basis (which reflects leverage as well as operating risk), financials and utilities show the lowest earnings volatility, while energy and communication services show the highest net income volatility.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The shift from technology (riskiest on operating income) to communication services (riskiest on net income) shows how capital structure/leverage reshuffles sector risk rankings once you move from operating to bottom-line earnings.
insights.locator: On a net income basis, financials and utilities have the lowest volatility

insights.claim: Utilities have the lowest percentage of money-losing firms of any sector globally, while healthcare and technology have the highest percentage of loss-making firms.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: High loss-making rates in healthcare and tech are a structural feature of these sectors, relevant when assessing survivorship risk in unprofitable growth names.
insights.locator: Utilities are again the least risky sector

insights.claim: Using the high-low price range as a volatility proxy, utilities and real estate are tied as the safest sectors globally in 2025, while healthcare has the widest price ranges of any sector.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Real estate joins utilities as a low-price-volatility sector even though it wasn't singled out in the accounting-based rankings, suggesting price-based and accounting-based measures can diverge at the margin.
insights.locator: Utilities again come in as safest

insights.claim: Measured by standard deviation of price changes, financials and utilities are the two safest sectors while technology and healthcare are the riskiest sectors globally in 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Tech and healthcare investors should expect structurally higher price volatility (and correspondingly higher hurdle rates) than the market average, independent of which risk metric is used.
insights.locator: Financials and utilities are the two safest sectors

insights.claim: Sector risk rankings converge across accounting and price-based measures - sectors with less earnings volatility and fewer money-losers also tend to show less stock price volatility, smaller price ranges, and lower betas - meaning the debate over which risk measure to use matters less than commonly assumed for relative sector comparisons.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors can be less dogmatic about picking a single 'correct' risk metric for cross-sector comparisons since accounting and market-based measures largely agree; the choice matters more for absolute hurdle-rate levels than for relative ranking.
insights.locator: you can see why the heated debates about which risk measure to use

insights.claim: Hurdle rates should be treated as opportunity costs - the returns investors or businesses could earn elsewhere on investments of equivalent risk - rather than numbers derived from personal risk aversion or gut feel.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Firms setting hurdle rates from internal policy or 'management comfort' rather than market-derived opportunity cost risk over- or under-investing relative to what the market would require.
insights.quote: hurdle rates are opportunity costs, reflecting returns that investors (businesses) can earn in the market on investments of equivalent risk
insights.locator: There are some investors and businesses who believe that hurdle rates come from their guts

insights.claim: Consistency is required between return metric and hurdle rate: equity-based returns (ROE or equity-cashflow IRR) must be compared to the cost of equity, while returns on invested capital or firm-cashflow IRR must be compared to the cost of capital.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Mismatching return and discount-rate bases (a common error in corporate capital budgeting and equity research) will systematically misjudge whether a project or company creates value.
insights.locator: As to the question of which of these hurdle rates you should use

insights.claim: Damodaran's implied equity risk premium approach to estimating the cost of equity is deliberately model-agnostic, designed to reflect what the market is actually pricing stocks to earn on an annual basis rather than relying on any single risk-and-return model.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: An implied-premium-based cost of equity updates with market pricing in real time, making it more responsive than static historical-premium approaches when markets reprice risk quickly.
insights.locator: my use of an implied equity risk premium in the estimation of the cost of equity

themes.theme: Company Age vs. Cost of Capital

insights.claim: Despite the intuitive expectation that younger firms should have higher costs of equity due to greater business uncertainty, Damodaran's decile analysis of firm age across the full sample found no discernible pattern in cost of equity across age classes.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume young/newly public companies automatically warrant a materially higher cost of equity purely for being young; age-related uncertainty may be diversifiable and thus not priced into equity risk.
insights.quote: there is no discernible pattern on costs of equity, as you go across the age classes
insights.locator: As you can see, there is no discernible pattern on costs of equity

insights.claim: As firms age they take on more debt (their earnings capacity to support debt grows), and this - rather than cost of equity - is what drives a modest rise in cost of capital, especially among the oldest firms in the market.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The age effect on overall cost of capital operates through capital-structure/leverage choices, not through perceived business risk, implying older, more-levered firms could see cost-of-capital increases if borrowing costs rise even without any change in operating risk.
insights.locator: as firms age, they do borrow more

themes.theme: Cost of Capital Benchmarks as a Practical Valuation Tool

insights.claim: At the start of 2026, 80% of all US companies have dollar costs of capital between 5.26% and 9.88%, and 80% of all global companies fall between 6.28% and 11.66%; Damodaran uses this distribution as a plausibility check, flagging a US company with a 14% dollar cost of capital as 'suspect' since it sits above the 90th percentile.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts can use these percentile bands as an immediate sanity check on any DCF's discount rate assumption before digging into the underlying beta/premium inputs - a cost of capital outside these bands demands justification.
insights.locator: This table is one on my most-used

insights.claim: When valuing a small, newly public, globally-operating AI company in US dollars, Damodaran's own practice is to start the valuation with a cost of capital of 11.66% and glide it down toward 8.65% as the company grows and matures.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, reusable numeric anchor for anyone modeling early-stage AI IPOs: start near the high end of the global distribution (~11.66%) and taper toward the market average (~8.65%) over the projection period.
insights.quote: I will start the valuation with a cost of capital of 11.66% and move that cost of capital over time towards 8.65%, as its gets larger and more established
insights.locator: if I am valuing a small, AI firm that has just gone public

insights.claim: To convert a dollar-based cost of capital table into another currency, add the expected inflation differential between the two currencies (e.g., roughly 1.76 percentage points using IMF forecasts of 4.00% Indian inflation versus 2.24% US inflation) directly to each dollar cost-of-capital figure.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives analysts a quick rule-of-thumb to localize Damodaran's dollar-denominated cost-of-capital tables for emerging-market valuations without rebuilding the whole model in local currency.
insights.locator: if you use the expected inflation rates of 2.24% and 4.00%

insights.claim: Entering 2026 marks the fourth consecutive year of US dollar risk-free rates holding around 4%, and the shock effect of the 2022 risk-free-rate surge - which had pushed costs of capital up broadly - appears to have faded as companies and investors have acclimatized to this rate regime.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A four-year-stable ~4% risk-free rate suggests discount-rate-driven repricing shocks (like 2022's valuation compression) are less likely near-term unless rates make a fresh directional move.
insights.locator: we are now in our fourth year with US dollar riskfree rates around 4%
